back: Oh! My mother is also from Estonia!

front: My mother is also from Estonia! (Finnish)

back: Hän on kotoisin Norjasta. Hän on norjalainen.

Do you speak Spanish?
(Note: -ko/-kö is the question suffix)

Do you live in Helsinki?
(Note: -ssä is the Inessive case 'in')

I am from Oulu.
(Note: -sta is the Elative case 'from')

(Note: negation uses 'ei' + verb in basic form)

What language does he/she speak?
(Note: 'mitä' is the partitive of 'mikä')

I am at work in Vantaa.
(Note: -llä is the Adessive case 'on/at')
There are two official languages in Finland.

I speak a little Spanish.
(Note: 'vähän' = a little)

front: In Finland, there are two official languages: Finnish and Swedish.
back: Suomessa on kaksi virallista kieltä: suomi ja ruotsi.

He speaks a little German.
Hän puhuu vähän saksaa.

front: I am at work in Vantaa.

back: How are you? (lit. What belongs to you?)

front: translate: 'he is our new colleague, Antti.'

(oppikirja = textbook)

What would you like (to have)?

It comes to 15 euros.
(Used when stating a total price)

front: Translate: 'I would like a coffee and a bun.'
Grammar Rule: How do you make a polite request in Finnish?
back:
Use the conditional mood (-isi-) or a question form.
Example: Ottaisin... / Saisinko...

back:
Do you eat fish?

Note: -ko/-kö is the interrogative suffix.

I'll just have coffee.

Note: 'ottaa' is used here to mean 'to take/order'.

Can we have the bill/check?

Note: -ko suffix makes it a question.
Are you paying together or separately?
Are you paying by card or in cash?
Do you have a free table for two?
Do you want to order something to drink already?

I would take/have a tomato soup.

Note: 'ottaisin' is the conditional form (I would take).

What are you eating?

Note: Finnish uses the present tense for both 'eat' and 'am eating'.
I will pay for the wine.
I am starving (literally: I have a terrible hunger).

front: Syödä (verb type 2) - minä syön, sinä syöt, hän syö
back: we eat (me syömme), you eat (te syötte), they eat (he syövät)

front: We will order a bottle of house red wine and also water.
back: Ottaisimme pullon talon punaviiniä ja lisäksi vettä.

It is half past one (halfway to two).
Note: In Finnish, 'puoli kaksi' means 1:30.

It is a quarter to five.
Note: 'Vaille' takes the genitive case (varttia).
Until what time is the pharmacy open?

It closes at six.
Note: The ablative case (-lta/-ltä) is used to indicate the time an action occurs.
At what time does the train leave?

front: Maanantai, tiistai, keskiviikko, torstai, perjantai, lauantai, sunnuntai
back: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday

front: Onko posti auki viiteen vai kuuteen asti?
back: Is the post office open until five or until six?
At what time does the post office close?

It is ten to four.
Note: 'Vaille' uses the partitive case (kymmentä).

front: It is a little past four.
It is a little before four.
The grocery store / The library / The shopping center
The pharmacy / The shoemaker / The laundry
When does the Finnish lesson start?
Does the bank open at ten?
Does the shop close at six?
The post office is open until six.
The post office closes at six.

back:
I would like the salmon soup.

back:

(Note: tytär = daughter)

front: He ovat naimisissa ja heillä on pieni vauva.
back: They are married and they have a small baby.

Tomi is Ulla's son.
(Note: Genitive case -n)

Who is in your family?
(Note: kuuluu = belongs to/is part of)

My wife and dog are part of my family.
(Note: Perheeseeni = into/to my family)

front: Avoliitto tarkoittaa, että pari asuu yhdessä, vaikka ei ole naimisissa.
back: Cohabitation means that a couple lives together, even though they are not married.

Perheeseen kuuluu...
(Note: verb is in singular even if multiple people follow)

front: The cohabiting relationship / living together without marriage

back:
He has ...
(Possessive structure: On him/her is ...)

(Note: 'hänen' is the genitive of 'hän')

front: Hänellä on tumma vai vaalea tukka?

back:
Millaiset hiukset sinulla on?

Hänellä on siniset silmät.

front: I think that they are blue.
back:
Luulen, että ne ovat siniset.
(Note: 'luulen' = I think/suppose)

What day is it today?
(Note: 'Mikä' asks for the identity of the day)

What is the date today?
(Literally: 'Which-ordinal day is it today?')

Today is the 11th of June.
(Note: Ordinal numbers are used for dates)

My birthday is the 8th of February.
(Note: The date uses the partitive case for the month)
Finland's Independence Day is the 6th of December.

front: Grammar: Why is it 'kahdeksas helmikuuta' and not 'kahdeksas helmikuu'?
back: When stating a date (Day + Month), the month must be in the **Partitive case**.
Jukka's birthday is the 7th of October.

kahdeskymmenes kesäkuu
tai kahdeskymmentä ensimmäinen kesäkuuta (context dependent, but usually 20. kesäkuuta)

to be windy / it is blowing

Is it cold today?

It is freezing outside.
(Case: Partitive of 'pakkanen')

Today it is sunny and 10 degrees of frost.
(Grammar: Partitive case used with weather adjectives/nouns)

In spring the sun shines and the snow melts.
(Verb: sulata -> sulaa)

(Grammar: 'Voi olla' = can/may be)

front: Translate: 'It is windy in the morning.'
translate: 'it is a heatwave / very hot.'
translate: 'it is a beautiful winter day.'
translate: 'it is often snow and frost.'

back: to visit / to go to (a place)

Do you visit/go to the shop?
(Note: -ssa indicates being inside)
Use **-ssa** for indoor locations (e.g., kaupassa, museossa) and **-lla** for open spaces or specific locations like market squares (e.g., torilla, kuntosalilla).
I can make food. I am making salmon soup.

front: joka päivä / usein / harvoin / joskus / ei koskaan
back: every day / often / rarely / sometimes / never

front: Mitä hän tekee? / Mitä he tekevät?
back: What is he/she doing? / What are they doing?

He sells cheese at the market square every day.
back: Hän myy torilla joka päivä juustoa.

front: He sometimes drinks tea in the cafe.

back: En voi, mutta voimme mennä ravintolaan.

back: Hän käy joka päivä aamulla uimahallissa.

back:
The sofa is in the living room.

front: Tässä on keittiö. Keittiössä on kello.
back:
Here is the kitchen. In the kitchen, there is a clock.
(Grammar: 'In [place] there is [thing]' uses the inessive case)

front: Tämä on omakotitalo. Talossa on autotalli.
back: This is a detached house. In the house, there is a garage.

front: Translate: 'The painting is in the bedroom.'

back:
in front of / behind
(Note: these use the genitive case)

beside (next to) / between
(Note: these use the genitive case)

on the right side / on the left side
(Note: these use the genitive case)

on top of / under
(Note: these use the genitive case)

The remote control is on the table.
(Note: '-llä' is the adessive case indicating surface)

I can't find it!
(Note: 'sitä' is the partitive form of 'se')

Have you seen my phone?
(Note: 'nähnyt' is the past participle used in perfect tense)

It is between the sofa cushions.
(Note: 'välissä' uses genitive plural)

Where is my phone now then?
(Note: 'nyt sitten' adds emphasis to the question)

I am looking from the floor...
(Note: 'etsin' is 1st person singular of 'etsiä')

front: At the edge of / At the table
The rug is on the floor, in front of the sofa, under the table.
back: Matto on lattialla sohvan edessä, pöydän alla.

front: Mom! Where is my school backpack?
Honey! Have you seen my phone?
I thought it was in the kitchen, but I can't find it.
back: Luulin, että se on keittiössä, mutta en löydä sitä.

front: Where is my phone now, then?
back: Missä minun puhelin nyt sitten on?

front: I've already looked, but it is not there.
back: Katsoin jo, mutta ei se ole siellä.

(välissä = in between)

(rautatieasema + -lle = to the railway station)

front: The hotel is next to the church.

The school is between the church and the hotel.

back:
advise / help / to go / to depart

intersection / street corner / traffic lights
(Note: these are common nouns for directions)

straight / forward / through / over
(Note: directional adverbs)

Turn to the left and continue straight.

front: the cinema is there (on the right/left)

back:
pähkinöitä / laktoosia / gluteenia (nuts / lactose / gluten)

Type: cloze

kortilla (by card)
---

tuoretta (fresh)
---
*Grammar: Partitive case used for uncountable/unspecified quantities.*

to like
(Note: requires the elative case -sta/-stä)

I like strawberries and chocolate.
(Note: -sta/-stä is the elative case)

What food do you like?
(Note: 'Mistä' is the ablative/elative form of 'mikä')

What is your favorite food?

He/she likes soups.
(Note: plural elative case)

How do you prepare the meatballs?
(Note: 'valmistat' from verb 'valmistaa')

I can give it to you!
(Note: 'voin' is 1st person singular of 'voida')

Mix everything carefully.
(Note: 'sekoita' is the imperative form)

Fry the meatballs in butter on a pan.
(Note: 'paista' = to fry/bake)

Serve the meatballs with mashed potatoes.
(Note: 'kanssa' = with)

Do you like fish food or vegetable food more?
(Note: '-vai' is used in questions to mean 'or')

back: Kastele kädet kylmällä vedellä ja muotoile lihapullat.

front: Translate: 'Serve the meatballs with mashed potatoes and lingonberry jam.'
back: Tarjoile lihapullat perunamuusin ja puolukkahillon kanssa.

Think about it more! / Consider it further!
(Imperative form)

Am I disturbing you?
(Verb Type 5 question form: -nko)

back:

(iso = big, pieni = small)

Unfortunately, we don't have size 42 left.

(lainata = to borrow)

(ostaa = to buy)

(haluta = to want)

front: Minua, sinua, häntä, meitä, teitä, heitä
back:
Me, you (sg), him/her, us, you (pl), them
(Note: Personal pronouns in the Partitive case)

back:

(to -lle: allative case)

(millä: with what/by what)

(lohta: partitive case)

front: How much does the salmon cost?
How do you get to work? (What [mode of transport] do you go to work with?)
Who are you waiting for under the clock?
You are in a clothing store and you are looking for jeans.
What kind of ice cream do you choose at the ice cream kiosk?
The sofa is in the living room.
How do I get from the campus to your workplace?

back:
I am going to Carlo's (place).
Grammar: Genitive + luokse = to someone's place.

I am at Carlo's (place).
Grammar: Genitive + luona = at someone's place.

I am coming from Carlo's (place).
Grammar: Genitive + luota = from someone's place.

Come around seven.
Phrase: [Time] + maissa = around/at approximately [time].
Does it suit you? / Is it okay for you?
It suits me. / That works for me.

To spend (time).
Example: viettää illan = to spend the evening.

We are coming to your place to visit.
Note: 'kylään' (to visit) is used with 'tulla/mennä'.
Are you coming with the children or alone?
I will come gladly / with pleasure.
Can I come with the children?
Give my regards to Carlo! / Greetings to Carlo!

front: at around six / around six o'clock

back: You can get to us by bus 25.

front: I am going to the movies with Jani tomorrow.
I am celebrating my birthday next Sunday.

back: Tell me about it! / You can say that again!
Come in / Come further in. (Polite plural/formal)
Let's eat / Let's sit at the table. (Literally: 'Let's go to the table')
Thanks for the last one! (Used when leaving/finishing a meal or event)
I must drive home. (Verb: ajaa - to drive)
He/she is a quite demanding boss. (Adj: vaativa - demanding)
I have to do overtime often. (Noun: ylitöitä - overtime)
Come visit us soon. (Noun: kylään - to [someone's] house/visit)
Thanks for the invitation, I'd love to come. (Noun: kutsusta - from/for the invitation)
It smells wonderful. (Adjective: ihana -> case form: ihanalta - from/of something wonderful)

How are you?
Not very well. (Partitive case of hyvä)

front: I'll fetch our main course now.
Do you still enjoy your work?

back: Is the cleaning of the space included in the price?
I will pay now. / Shall I send the invoice?
I am organizing a colleague's farewell party.
Could (you) send the offer to the email?
The order must be made no later than Sept 16th.

front: Is the cleaning of the space included in the price?
The keys must be returned at 11 PM.
Will you order the food from us or bring your own?
back: Tilaatteko ruuat meiltä vai tuotteko omat?

front: I can send an offer to your email.
Unfortunately, no. The space is already booked.
back: Valitettavasti ei. Tila on jo varattu.

front: Celebratory guests / party guests are coming.

back: birthday person / hero of the day
Is the gift from Matti or from Petri?

How is he/she?
(Literally: What belongs to him/her?)
May I have permission? / May I?

I will bring you coffee.
(Verb: tuoda - to bring)
Tell mother greetings / Say hi to mom.

Matti writes a poem to Liisa.
(Grammar: Allative -lle indicates recipient)

I ask for help from the waiter.
(Case: Ablative -lta/ltä = from)

front: I will sing a serenade for her.
I will introduce my colleague to you.
He gets an award from the academy tomorrow.
Have a nice evening, you all!

back: You have a meeting with Carlo.
The receptionist directs you to the lobby.
Do you have your own office?
I have a workstation in the open-plan office.
The photocopier is there in the hallway.
Let's go to the meeting room (slang/colloquial).

front: Do you work in an open-plan office?
Is the coffee machine in the lobby or in the meeting room?
The receptionist directs you to the lobby.

back:
to go to work
(literally: to go to work)
Where are you going? (to what place?)

Jari tells/talks about the project.
(Grammar: -sta/-stä is the Elative case, used here for 'about')

Before the meeting / After the meeting
(Grammar: Ennen + Partitive | Jälkeen + Genitive)

He speaks to the customer.
(Verb: puhua)

Jukka sits at the desk.
(Verb: istua)
What do you chat about with your colleagues?

front: The team discusses the project schedule and budget.
back: Tiimi keskustelee projektin aikataulusta ja budjetista.

front: He talks about sports and the weather.
Jukka is tired in the afternoon.
We talk about the summer holiday.

back:
Does it suit you (singular) / you (plural)?
Is it okay for you?
I am in a great hurry / I am very busy.

We meet at lunch / during lunch.
(Adessive case -lla used for time/location)

Is Monday at two o'clock okay?
(Ablative case -lta used for time)

I am meeting a client
(Present tense of 'tavata')

I will reserve a meeting room for us.
(Future intent using present tense)

Shall we meet at nine o'clock?
(-taan is the passive/impersonal form used for 'shall we')
Unfortunately, it doesn't suit / it is not possible.

front: The workday begins / The workday ends
We need to discuss the budget.
I am on a business trip in Tampere.
Does it suit you next week?
I am in training (at a training session).
A little later, for example at half past three?
Can we meet on Tuesday at one o'clock?

back:
Thank you for the message.
(Note: -stä is the elative case)

Sorry that I am only replying now.
(vastaan = I reply/answer)

If you need more information, don't hesitate.
(epäröi = hesitate)

You (plural/formal) can contact [us/me].
(ottaa yhteyttä = to contact)

front: Ovatko teidän tunnit verkossa vai kasvotusten?
back:
Are your classes online or face-to-face?
(kasvotusten = face-to-face)

I am writing an email to a colleague.
(työkaverille = to/for a colleague)

I miss you.
(lit: I have longing for you)

to inquire / to ask
minä tiedustelen
sinä tiedustelit
hän tiedustelee

To whom are you writing an email?

front: I am writing to you because...
I know the basics of Finnish, but I would like to learn more.
back: Osaan jo suomen alkeet mutta haluaisin oppia lisää.

We will go shopping before the boat trip.
back: Huomenna käymme vielä ostoksilla ennen laivamatkaa.

front: I hope you are doing well.

back:
to dress / to put on (clothes)

to fetch / to pick up

What do you do for a living? / What is your job?

back:
Invite a friend to visit!
(Kutsu = imperative/command form of kutsua)

Whose place are you going to visit?
(Kenen = whose, luokse = to/towards someone's place)

I would like to reserve the party space.
(Haluaisin = I would like, juhlatila = party room/space)

Happy birthday!
(Literally: Congratulations to the day's hero!)

What do you say when you raise a glass?
(nostaa = to lift/raise)

Do you work in an open-plan office?
(avokonttori = open-plan office)

What do you discuss with the boss?
(pomo = boss, keskustella = to discuss/chat)

Can we meet?
(voida = can/to be able to, tavata = to meet)

Let's agree on a time and place!
(sovia = to agree/arrange)

Who works in a hospital?
(työskennellä = to work, sairaala = hospital)

What is your typical everyday life like?
(arkipäivä = weekday/everyday life)

Does Monday at two o'clock suit?
(sopia = to fit/suit, kahdelta = from/at two)

front: Who are you going to with?
To whom do you give a gift (at the party)?
